narration.default: You've got enough pieces now to build a real game. The computer will think of a secret number and give the kid a hint — too high, too low, or correct. You already know if and else. Today you'll learn elif — the word that lets you chain THREE possible answers together in one decision.

Type the first line:

print("Guess the number!")

This just prints a title in the terminal when the game starts. Anything in quotes is just text to Python — your title can be whatever you like.

Leave a blank line, then type:

number = 5

This is your "secret" answer. It's not very secret right now — the kid can read the code. Real games hide the answer by having the computer pick a random number, but for now 5 is fine — it keeps our attention on the decision logic.

Leave a blank line and type:

guess = int(input("Enter your guess: "))

Same pattern as the Numbers tutorial — ask a question and wrap with int, because we're going to COMPARE it to number. Comparing works on real numbers, not text.

Notice the double equals sign in if guess == number. That's not a typo. One equals sign (=) means STORE — like age = 10 stores 10 under age. Two equals signs (==) means CHECK — "are these the same?". Mixing them up is the most common bug in early Python code. Read your if conditions carefully.

Click Run. Type 3. The terminal says Too low. Walk through what happened: the first if asked is 3 the same as 5 — false. The elif asked is 3 greater than 5 — false. The else caught everything else. Python ran exactly ONE branch.

Click Run again. Type 9. Now the terminal says Too high. This time the first if was still false, but the elif was true — 9 is greater than 5 — so Python ran that one and skipped the else. First-true-wins.
Click Run once more. Type 5. The terminal says Correct! The very first if was true, so Python ran it and didn't even LOOK at the elif or else. Three runs, three different messages, one program.
